meta.question: How should we build a remote permission and monitoring dashboard for Claude Code — a web UI that receives permission requests, shows live activity, and lets users approve/deny tool calls remotely over tunneled connections?

claims.content: Must integrate with Claude Code's existing hook and permission system — no forking or patching Claude Code itself.
Self-contained Node.js server with minimal dependencies. Browser-based UI, no native app required.
Remote access must be secure for short-term sessions — tunneled via ngrok, Cloudflare Tunnel, or Tailscale with authentication.
Dashboard must support: (1) permission request notifications with approve/deny, (2) live activity feed of Claude Code actions, (3) toggleable claim tree and sprint dashboard views.
Sprint must produce both a working prototype and an architecture recommendation document for production use.

Claude Code supports 18 hook events including PreToolUse and PermissionRequest. Both fire before tool execution and can return allow/deny decisions via JSON. HTTP hooks (type: 'http') POST event data to a URL and accept synchronous JSON responses — this is the native integration point for a remote dashboard.

PermissionRequest hooks receive permission_suggestions (the 'always allow' options users see) and can respond with decision.behavior='allow'|'deny' plus updatedPermissions. PreToolUse hooks can return permissionDecision='allow'|'deny'|'ask'. Both support updatedInput to modify tool arguments before execution.

Hook input (POST body) includes: session_id, transcript_path, cwd, permission_mode, tool_name, tool_input, tool_use_id, agent_id, agent_type. This gives a remote dashboard full context about what Claude is doing and why.

HTTP hook timeout defaults to 30 seconds (configurable up to 600s). Non-2xx responses or timeouts are non-blocking errors — execution continues. This means if the dashboard server is down, Claude Code doesn't hang, but permissions fall through to the local terminal.

Hook configuration lives in settings files: ~/.claude/settings.json (user-wide), .claude/settings.json (project), .claude/settings.local.json (personal, gitignored). HTTP hooks support header interpolation with allowedEnvVars for auth tokens.

PostToolUse and PostToolUseFailure hooks fire after execution and are non-blocking. These can feed the live activity stream — every tool call, success or failure, gets reported to the dashboard without affecting Claude Code's performance.

Hook JSON config is loaded at session startup. Mid-session changes require /hooks menu interaction or session restart. This means the dashboard can't dynamically reconfigure which tools need approval without restarting Claude Code.

Check Point Research found CVEs (CVE-2025-59536, CVE-2026-21852) where malicious .claude/settings.json enables RCE through hooks. Hook URLs in settings are plaintext. For our dashboard this means: the hook config itself is a security-sensitive file, and the localhost HTTP endpoint must be authenticated.

Ngrok provides TLS tunnels with ephemeral URLs, optional --auth (basic auth), and IP allowlisting on paid plans. Traffic routes through ngrok's infrastructure (trust dependency). Cloudflare Tunnel runs through your own CF account with Zero Trust access policies (email OTP, no client install). Tailscale uses WireGuard mesh VPN with ACLs — zero public exposure but requires client on both ends.

For short-term fixed sessions: ngrok with --auth is adequate. For anything longer or multi-user: Cloudflare Tunnel with Access policies preferred (free tier, email OTP auth, no trust dependency on third-party infra for inspection). Tailscale is best if both endpoints can install a client.

Recommended architecture: Node.js HTTP server on localhost receives hook POSTs from Claude Code. Server holds pending permission requests in memory. Browser connects via WebSocket for real-time push. User approves/denies in browser, server responds to the held HTTP request. PostToolUse hooks feed activity stream. claims.json watched via fs.watch for sprint dashboard updates.

Claude Code has 5 permission modes: default (prompt each tool), acceptEdits (auto-accept file edits), plan (read-only), dontAsk (deny unless pre-approved), bypassPermissions (skip all checks). The dashboard should be aware of the current mode and adjust its behavior — e.g., in acceptEdits mode, only non-edit tools trigger approval requests.

The PermissionRequest hook timeout (default 30s) is the window for a human to approve/deny remotely. If the user doesn't respond in time, the hook times out and falls through to local terminal prompt. For mobile/slow connections, may need to extend timeout to 120-300s, but this stalls Claude Code's entire session.

Several community projects wrap Claude Code as a subprocess for remote access (claude-code-server, claude-code-webui, CloudeCode, 247-claude-code-remote). None intercept the permission system remotely via hooks — they either pre-approve tools or relay stdin/stdout. Our HTTP hook approach would be novel.

Minimum viable prototype requires: (1) Node.js HTTP server ~200 LOC, (2) WebSocket push to browser ~100 LOC, (3) Browser UI with approve/deny + activity feed ~300 LOC HTML/JS, (4) Hook config in settings.json ~20 lines. Total: ~600 LOC, zero npm dependencies if using Node built-in http + ws modules.

Working prototype confirms: Node.js HTTP server successfully receives Claude Code hook POSTs, parses tool_name/tool_input/session_id, and returns allow/deny JSON. The hook contract works exactly as documented — zero surprises.

Prototype architecture: server.mjs (HTTP server + WebSocket) holds Claude Code's PermissionRequest response open while pushing the request to the browser via WebSocket. Browser clicks approve/deny, server relays the decision back as the HTTP response. Round-trip is synchronous from Claude Code's perspective.

Prototype has zero npm dependencies. Uses Node built-in http, fs, crypto modules plus a custom 130-line WebSocket server (ws.mjs) implementing RFC 6455 text frames. Total: ~550 lines across 3 files (server.mjs, ws.mjs, dashboard.html).

Dashboard UI has 4 tabs: (1) Permissions — pending approve/deny cards with tool context, browser Notification API for push alerts, (2) Activity — real-time feed of all tool calls via PostToolUse hooks, (3) Claims — filterable by topic with evidence tier bars, (4) Sprint — phase/type/evidence breakdowns with stats.

Prototype uses token-based auth: random 16-byte hex token generated on startup, required as query param for dashboard and WebSocket. Hook endpoints are unauthenticated (Claude Code doesn't send custom auth by default), but localhost-only. For remote access, tunnel auth (ngrok --auth or Cloudflare Access) provides the outer layer.

120-second timeout for held permission requests is a tradeoff: too short and mobile users can't respond in time; too long and Claude Code's session stalls. Fallthrough on timeout sends empty JSON (no decision), which should let Claude Code fall back to local terminal prompt.
